title: C# Advanced — Paper 10
description: Advanced C# covering Async Patterns & CancellationToken, Generics, Generic Variance, LINQ Core & Advanced Operators, Records/Tuples/Deconstruction, Advanced Exception Handling, Memory Management, Span<T>/Memory<T>, and Reflection.

questions.text: What is the difference between a positional record and a nominal record in C# 9+?

options.text: Positional records are value types; nominal records are reference types.

A positional record uses a primary constructor to declare properties concisely: 'record Point(int X, int Y)'; a nominal record uses standard property declarations with init accessors. Both are functionally equivalent but positional syntax is more concise.
Nominal records support inheritance; positional records do not.
Positional records are immutable; nominal records are always mutable.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the CancellationTokenSource.CancelAfter() method?

options.text: It cancels all registered tokens after the source is disposed.
CancelAfter() schedules automatic cancellation after a specified delay (TimeSpan or milliseconds), enabling timeout-based cancellation without manually creating a timer.
CancelAfter() is equivalent to Thread.Sleep() followed by Cancel().
CancelAfter() cancels any downstream linked tokens only.

questions.text: What is the 'notnull' generic constraint in C# 8+ and what does it enforce?

options.text: notnull constrains T to value types only.

The 'notnull' constraint ensures T is a non-nullable type (either a non-nullable reference type or a value type); with nullable reference types enabled, it prevents nullable reference types from being used as T.
notnull automatically adds null checks to all method calls on T.
notnull is equivalent to the 'struct' constraint.

questions.text: What is the Func<in T, out TResult> contravariant input and covariant output in practice?

options.text: Func<T,TResult> is invariant in both T and TResult.

Func<T,TResult> is contravariant in T (in) and covariant in TResult (out): Func<object,string> can be assigned to Func<string,object> because it accepts any object (handles strings) and returns a string (which is an object).
Func<T,TResult> is covariant in T and contravariant in TResult.
Variance does not apply to Func<T,TResult>.

questions.text: What is LINQ's Except() operator and what does it return?

options.text: Except() returns elements present in both sequences.
Except() returns elements from the first sequence that are not present in the second sequence (set difference), using the default equality comparer or a provided one.
Except() excludes the first N elements of a sequence.
Except() removes duplicates from a single sequence.

questions.text: What does LINQ's Intersect() operator do?

options.text: Intersect() returns elements that are in the first sequence but not the second.
Intersect() returns the set intersection: elements that appear in both sequences, using the default equality comparer or a provided one.
Intersect() concatenates two sequences and removes duplicates.
Intersect() returns the union of both sequences.

questions.text: How does C# 12 collection expressions work and how do they relate to existing collection types?

options.text: Collection expressions only work with arrays.

C# 12 collection expressions use '[1, 2, 3]' syntax to initialize any collection type that supports it (arrays, List<T>, Span<T>, ImmutableArray<T>, etc.); the compiler picks the optimal initialization based on the target type.
Collection expressions always create a new List<T>.
Collection expressions require the CollectionBuilder attribute.

questions.text: What is the checked/unchecked context for arithmetic in C# and what is the default?

options.text: The default context is checked; overflow throws OverflowException by default.
The default arithmetic context is unchecked; integer overflow silently wraps. The 'checked' keyword/block enables overflow detection, throwing OverflowException on integer overflow.
Checked is the default for floating-point; unchecked for integers.
Both checked and unchecked are compile-time only; there is no runtime difference.

questions.text: What is GC.Collect() used for and when is it appropriate to call it?

options.text: GC.Collect() should be called frequently to optimize memory usage.

GC.Collect() triggers an explicit garbage collection; it should rarely be called in production code as it interrupts normal GC scheduling. Legitimate uses include after a known large allocation release or in test/diagnostic scenarios.
GC.Collect() compacts the managed heap.
GC.Collect() is required to free objects that implement IDisposable.

questions.text: What is the purpose of ArrayPool<T>.Shared in .NET?

options.text: ArrayPool<T>.Shared provides a single shared array of type T.

ArrayPool<T>.Shared is a shared pool of reusable arrays; you Rent(size) to get an array (may be larger than requested) and Return() it when done, dramatically reducing GC pressure for short-lived large arrays.
ArrayPool<T>.Shared is only safe to use on a single thread.
ArrayPool<T>.Shared always returns zero-initialized arrays.

questions.text: What is the difference between Memory<T>.Span and Memory<T> itself?

options.text: Memory<T>.Span is a different type stored on the heap.

Memory<T>.Span returns a Span<T> over the same memory region; Span<T> is a ref struct (stack-only) while Memory<T> is a regular struct that can be stored as a field, passed to async methods, and used across await points.
Memory<T>.Span copies the data to a new stack allocation.
They are the same type; .Span is just an alias.

questions.text: What is reflection emit and how does it differ from expression tree compilation?

options.text: They produce identical IL code and are interchangeable.

Reflection.Emit (System.Reflection.Emit) allows generating full IL byte code for dynamic types, methods, and assemblies at runtime with fine-grained control; expression tree compilation is higher-level, limited to expressions (not arbitrary statements), but easier to use.
Expression trees are lower-level than Reflection.Emit.
Reflection.Emit is deprecated; expression trees replace it entirely.

questions.text: What is a generic interface default implementation in C# 8+ and what restrictions apply?

options.text: Default interface methods are only allowed on abstract interfaces.

C# 8+ allows interface methods to have a default implementation body; classes implementing the interface inherit the default if not overridden. Restrictions: default implementations require the CLR >=.NET 5 (or compatible), and they are accessed via the interface type, not the class type.
Default interface implementations replace abstract classes entirely.
Default interface methods can be sealed by the implementing class.

questions.text: How does reflection handle generic methods and how do you invoke a generic method with a specific type argument?

options.text: Generic methods cannot be invoked via reflection.
Get the MethodInfo for the open generic method, then call MakeGenericMethod(typeof(T)) to get a closed MethodInfo, then call .Invoke(instance, args) with the appropriate arguments.
You must specify generic arguments in GetMethod() directly.
Generic methods are invoked without specifying type arguments; the runtime infers them.

questions.text: What is the IAsyncEnumerable<T> cancellation pattern with WithCancellation()?

options.text: WithCancellation() is called on IAsyncEnumerable<T> to replace the source.

Calling asyncEnumerable.WithCancellation(token) returns a configured IAsyncEnumerable<T> that passes the token to the underlying GetAsyncEnumerator(token), enabling the producer to observe the token and stop early.
WithCancellation() converts an IAsyncEnumerable into a Task<List<T>>.
WithCancellation() automatically cancels after a fixed timeout.

questions.text: What is a covariant return type on a record clone method ('protected virtual T Clone()') in C#?

options.text: Records do not have a Clone method.

The compiler generates a 'protected virtual T Clone()' method on records used for 'with' expressions; in derived records, this is overridden with a covariant return type returning the derived record type, ensuring 'with' produces the correct derived type.
Clone() on records is a public static method.
The Clone method uses MemberwiseClone() and returns object.

questions.text: What is the LINQ Union() operator and how does it differ from Concat()?

options.text: Union() and Concat() return identical results.
Union() returns distinct elements from both sequences (set union, no duplicates); Concat() appends the second sequence to the first without removing duplicates.
Concat() removes duplicates; Union() keeps all duplicates.
Union() requires both sequences to be of the same concrete type.

questions.text: What is the difference between Marshal.AllocHGlobal() and stackalloc in unsafe C#?

options.text: They are interchangeable; both allocate unmanaged memory.

Marshal.AllocHGlobal() allocates unmanaged heap memory that persists until Marshal.FreeHGlobal() is called; stackalloc allocates on the stack and is automatically freed when the method exits. Stack allocations are faster but size-limited.
stackalloc allocates on the heap; Marshal.AllocHGlobal() allocates on the stack.
Marshal.AllocHGlobal() is managed by the GC; stackalloc is not.

questions.text: What is a ValueTask<T> backed by IValueTaskSource<T> and when is it used?

options.text: IValueTaskSource<T> is only used in the .NET runtime itself, not in user code.

IValueTaskSource<T> allows pooling the ValueTask<T>'s backing state (instead of allocating a new Task<T> each time), enabling zero-allocation async patterns for high-performance scenarios like I/O on hot paths.
IValueTaskSource<T> is equivalent to TaskCompletionSource<T>.
ValueTask<T> backed by IValueTaskSource<T> cannot be awaited multiple times.

questions.text: What is the purpose of MemoryMarshal.Cast<TFrom, TTo>() in .NET?

options.text: MemoryMarshal.Cast reinterprets the type of elements in a span.

MemoryMarshal.Cast<TFrom,TTo>(Span<TFrom>) reinterprets the raw bytes of the span as a different element type without copying, e.g., treating a Span<byte> as Span<int> to read integer data from a byte buffer.
MemoryMarshal.Cast converts managed memory to unmanaged memory.
Both a and b are correct.

questions.text: What is a generic variance constraint combining 'in' and 'out' on different type parameters?

options.text: A single interface cannot have both in and out type parameters.

An interface can have both: e.g., interface IConverter<in TInput, out TOutput> { TOutput Convert(TInput input); } TInput is contravariant (input only), TOutput is covariant (output only), so IConverter<Animal,string> can be used as IConverter<Dog,object>.
Combining in and out cancels both, making the parameters invariant.
Both in and out make the type parameter bidirectional.

questions.text: What is a LINQ query continuation using 'into' in query syntax?

options.text: 'into' in LINQ terminates the query early.

'into' is used after 'select' or 'group' to introduce a new range variable for a query continuation, allowing further operations (where, select, orderby) on the projected results without starting a new query.
'into' is used for inserting items into a collection via LINQ.
'into' is only valid with the join clause.

questions.text: What is the difference between NullReferenceException and ArgumentNullException and when should each be thrown?

options.text: They are equivalent; use either for null-related errors.

NullReferenceException is thrown by the runtime when dereferencing a null; ArgumentNullException should be thrown explicitly by library code to validate that a required argument is not null, giving a clearer error message with the parameter name.
ArgumentNullException is thrown by the runtime; NullReferenceException is user-thrown.
Neither should ever be thrown manually; use Debug.Assert instead.

questions.text: What is a Span<T> over a string (via AsSpan()) and what does it enable?

options.text: string.AsSpan() copies the string into a new char array.

string.AsSpan() returns a ReadOnlySpan<char> over the string's underlying character buffer without copying, enabling efficient substring operations, character-level parsing, and zero-allocation string manipulation.
string.AsSpan() enables mutating the string's characters in place.
string.AsSpan() is only available for StringBuilder.

questions.text: What is Type.MakeGenericType() used for in reflection?

options.text: It creates an instance of a generic type.

Type.MakeGenericType(typeof(T)) constructs a closed generic type from an open generic type definition at runtime: e.g., typeof(List<>).MakeGenericType(typeof(int)) returns typeof(List<int>), which can then be used with Activator.CreateInstance.
It defines a new generic type at runtime.
MakeGenericType is used to add type parameters to an existing closed type.

questions.text: What is an async enumerable producer that uses ConfigureAwait(false) and why does it matter?

options.text: ConfigureAwait(false) cannot be used inside async iterators.

Inside an async iterator, each 'await' can use ConfigureAwait(false) to avoid capturing the synchronization context; this is important for library code producers that should not marshal continuations back to a specific context (e.g., UI thread).
ConfigureAwait(false) on an async iterator makes it run synchronously.
ConfigureAwait(false) only matters when the consumer calls GetAsyncEnumerator().

questions.text: What is the difference between an exception filter (when) and catching then re-throwing?

options.text: They are semantically identical; the filter is only a style preference.

An exception filter (when) evaluates before stack unwinding; if the condition is false the stack is not unwound and debuggers show the original throw site. Catch-then-rethrow unwinds the stack before the check, losing the original call context if the exception is re-thrown via 'throw ex'.
Exception filters only work in async methods.
Re-throwing with bare 'throw' and using a filter have identical debugger behavior.

questions.text: What is the purpose of the Span<T>.TryCopyTo() method?

options.text: TryCopyTo() copies elements one-by-one with error recovery.

TryCopyTo(destination) attempts to copy all elements to the destination Span<T>; it returns true on success and false if the destination is too short, without throwing an exception, enabling safe copy operations with a capacity check.
TryCopyTo() copies as many elements as fit and returns the count.
TryCopyTo() is only available on Memory<T>, not Span<T>.

questions.text: What is the tuple swap idiom in C# 7+ using deconstruction?

options.text: Tuples cannot be used to swap variables.
(a, b) = (b, a); uses tuple deconstruction to swap two variables atomically without a temporary variable, as the right-hand side tuple is created before the left-hand side assignment.
The swap requires creating a named tuple type first.
(a, b) = (b, a) is only valid for value types.

questions.text: What does GC.KeepAlive() do and when is it necessary?

options.text: GC.KeepAlive() pins the object in memory permanently.

GC.KeepAlive(obj) ensures the GC considers the object reachable until the call point, preventing premature collection in scenarios where the only live reference is used before this point (e.g., in P/Invoke when a delegate's lifetime must extend to cover the native callback).
GC.KeepAlive() increments the object's reference count.
GC.KeepAlive() adds the object to gen 2 permanently.
